name: build-specs
description: Decompose an underspecified slice into its independent components and write a detailed handoff spec per component (one per fan-out worker).

input.description: a plan slice or feature to elaborate into detailed, implementable specs

output.description: a handoff spec: one precise spec per component, with contracts, acceptance criteria, and exact values

nodes.prompt:
You are about to write detailed implementation specs for the given plan slice. Your ONLY job in this step is to judge readiness — do not write any spec or file yet. FIRST decide whether the slice is concrete enough to spec, or whether something ESSENTIAL is missing — the scope, which area or files it touches, the interfaces it must meet, or what 'done' looks like. Read available context (code, files) to resolve what you can yourself before asking.

End your reply with EXACTLY one of these labels on its own final line:
READY     — concrete enough to spec.
NEED_INFO — something essential is missing. BEFORE the label, list 2-4 SPECIFIC blocking questions the user must answer — only genuine blockers.

Break the slice into the genuinely INDEPENDENT components that each deserve their own spec — a module, an endpoint, a data shape, a UI piece. Read the relevant code so each names a real seam. Keep it to the genuinely separate pieces (usually a handful, 2-5); never split one function into sub-aspects.

For each component, pick a SHORT, distinctive slug (e.g. "auth-module", "rate-limiter"). The slug is that component's exact name: it reappears verbatim as the component's section heading and is checked for coverage, so keep it short and unambiguous.

Produce two JSON arrays over the SAME components, in the SAME order:
1. Your structured output — the items list, each string shaped "slug: one-sentence responsibility", the slug FIRST, then a colon, then one sentence on what that component must do. This is the whole task the fan-out worker for that component receives, so make the sentence carry real information.
2. A file named components.json in your working directory — an array of the slugs ALONE, no responsibility text. These are the exact coverage keys checked later; each must match the slug half of the corresponding text-output item exactly.

Do NOT write the spec itself here — that is the very next step; here you only IDENTIFY the components.

items.description: One string per component, shaped 'slug: one-sentence responsibility'.

nodes.prompt: Write the detailed spec for the SINGLE component you were given. Read the surrounding code so it fits reality. Cover: the exact interface/contract (signatures, inputs, outputs, types), the behavior, the edge cases, and concrete acceptance criteria a reviewer could check — with exact values, not vague description. Be precise enough that an implementer builds it without guessing. Output just the spec for this component.

Combine the per-component specs above into ONE coherent handoff spec for the whole slice. Give each component its own section headed by that component's exact name, verbatim — an automated check afterward confirms every component is covered by name. Keep each component's contract and acceptance criteria; add a short overview and call out cross-component dependencies. This is what an implementation step will build against, so keep it precise and complete.

nodes.command:
python3 -c "import json,sys
try:
    comps=json.load(open('components.json'))
except (OSError, ValueError):
    sys.stderr.write('components.json missing or unreadable - the frame step failed to write it')
    sys.exit(1)
text=sys.stdin.read().lower()
missing=[c for c in comps if c.lower() not in text]
sys.stderr.write('\n'.join(missing))
sys.exit(1 if missing else 0)"
